title.en: Landing Zones & Organization Policy on GCP

description.en: Design the paved road: folder structures, Shared VPC, org policy constraints, and Terraform-driven project factories that make the secure path the easy path.

story_variants.en:
## Before the First Workload

Give fifty teams raw access to a cloud and you get fifty snowflake environments: public buckets, service account keys in git, VMs with external IPs in regions legal never approved. A **landing zone** is the antidote — the pre-built foundation of hierarchy, guardrails, networking, and identity into which workloads *land*. The goal is a paved road: the secure, compliant path is also the path of least resistance.

A GCP landing zone has four pillars:

1. **Resource hierarchy** — the org/folder/project layout from lesson 1, designed for policy attachment: environment folders (`prod`, `nonprod`, `sandbox`), a `shared-infra` folder for common services, and a project factory that stamps out new projects consistently.
2. **Identity** — Cloud Identity federated to your IdP, group-based IAM, no unmanaged accounts.
3. **Guardrails** — Organization Policy constraints (this lesson's core) plus IAM Deny and VPC Service Controls for data perimeters.
4. **Networking** — typically **Shared VPC**: a host project owns subnets; service projects attach workloads to them, so network and firewall governance is centralized while teams keep project autonomy.

> **Callout:** Google ships this as opinionated code — Fabric FAST and the Cloud Foundation Toolkit Terraform modules. Enterprises rarely hand-build landing zones anymore; they fork the blueprint and adapt. Infrastructure as code isn't an option here, it's the medium.

## Organization Policy: Rules About Resources, Not People

IAM answers *who can do what*. **Organization Policy** answers *what is allowed to exist at all* — regardless of who asks. An org policy sets a **constraint** at org, folder, or project level, and it binds even project owners.

The constraints every enterprise sets on day one:

| Constraint | Effect |
|---|---|
| `gcp.resourceLocations` | Resources may only be created in listed regions (data residency) |
| `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` | No downloadable SA keys — forces federation and attached identities |
| `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` | IAM grants restricted to your own domains — no gmail.com principals |
| `compute.vmExternalIpAccess` | Deny public IPs on VMs |
| `storage.publicAccessPrevention` | Buckets can never be made public |
| `compute.requireOsLogin` | SSH via IAM-governed OS Login only |

Constraints come in two shapes: **list constraints** (allow/deny lists of values, like regions) and **boolean constraints** (enforce on/off). **Custom organization policies** extend the system with CEL expressions over resource attributes — e.g., 'GKE clusters must have Workload Identity enabled.'

## Inheritance: The Part Everyone Gets Wrong Once

Org policies inherit down the hierarchy like IAM, but with a crucial difference: **a lower level can override or merge, if the policy allows it**. Each policy either inherits the parent value (default when unset), *replaces* it, or — for list constraints — *merges* with it (`inheritFromParent: true` plus additional values).

The evaluation rule for the unset case is what your challenge models: walk up from the resource toward the org; **the nearest ancestor that sets the constraint wins**. Org says regions `us, eu`; the `prod` folder tightens to `eu`; the project says nothing — the project's effective value is `eu`, from the folder. The `disableSAKeys` boolean set only at org reaches everything.

> **Pitfall:** Overriding to *loosen* is possible when the constraint permits it — and it's how 'temporary' sandbox exceptions become permanent shadow policy. Mature shops alert on any org-policy change (Audit Logs -> lesson 5's Pub/Sub -> ticket) and time-box exceptions with expiry-tagged tickets.

## The Project Factory Pattern

New project requests shouldn't be console clicks; they should be pull requests. A **project factory** is a Terraform module + CI pipeline: a team submits `team: recommendations, env: prod, budget: X` as YAML; review approves; the pipeline creates the project in the right folder with baseline IAM (groups, not users), Shared VPC attachment, mandatory labels (`cost-center`, `owner`, `data-class` — lesson 14 depends on these), logging sinks, and the org-policy inheritance that folder placement implies. Day-one compliance, zero heroics, and an audit trail that *is* the git history.

This is also where the AI story lands: a `ml-workloads` folder commonly carries its own policy layer — allowed regions matched to accelerator availability, Vertex AI service agents pre-granted, VPC-SC perimeter membership — so data scientists get GPUs inside guardrails rather than tickets and exceptions.

## What You'll Build

An org-policy inheritance resolver: given constraint settings at `org`, `folder:prod`, and `project:api-prod`, compute the project's effective policy using nearest-ancestor-wins, and report which level each value came from. It's the logic behind the console's 'effective policy' tab — and behind every 'why can't I create this bucket in asia-south1?' Slack thread you'll ever answer.

description.en: Four pillars, stamped out by a Terraform project factory.

left.label: IAM: 'Asha may create buckets in this project'

right.label: Org Policy: 'No bucket in this subtree may ever be public — even if created by an owner'

description.en: IAM governs identities; Org Policy governs what resources may exist. You need both.

explanation.en: The constraint is set at the prod folder — every project inside inherits it.
A list constraint with an allowed-values rule; value groups like in:eu-locations cover whole geographies.

description.en: Org policy denies at creation time, and tells you which ancestor set the rule — your resolver computes exactly this.
The project factory: new environments as code review, not console archaeology.

instruction.en: Build an org-policy inheritance resolver. POLICIES maps hierarchy level to the constraints it sets; CHAIN lists levels from root to leaf: ['org', 'folder:prod', 'project:api-prod']. A constraint's effective value at the leaf is the value set by the NEAREST level that sets it (walking leaf-ward; later levels in CHAIN override earlier ones). Implement effective_policy(chain, policies) returning {constraint: (value, source_level)}. Print 'project:api-prod effective policy:' then, for each constraint in sorted order, '<constraint> = <value> (set at <source_level>)'. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
POLICIES = {
    "org": {"allowedRegions": "us,eu", "disableSAKeys": "true"},
    "folder:prod": {"allowedRegions": "eu"},
    "project:api-prod": {},
}
CHAIN = ["org", "folder:prod", "project:api-prod"]

def effective_policy(chain, policies):
    # TODO: walk chain root->leaf; each level's settings override earlier ones.
    #       Track BOTH the value and which level set it.
    #       Return {constraint: (value, source_level)}
    pass

eff = effective_policy(CHAIN, POLICIES)
print("project:api-prod effective policy:")
# TODO: for each constraint in sorted(eff):
#   print '<constraint> = <value> (set at <source_level>)'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
project:api-prod effective policy:
allowedRegions = eu (set at folder:prod)
disableSAKeys = true (set at org)

en: Build a dict as you walk CHAIN in order: for each level, for each (constraint, value) it sets, store (value, level) — later levels naturally overwrite earlier ones.
allowedRegions is set at org AND folder:prod — the folder is nearer the leaf, so 'eu' from 'folder:prod' wins. disableSAKeys is only set at org.
sorted(eff) yields allowedRegions before disableSAKeys; unpack value, source = eff[c] for the f-string.

practice_challenges.test_code:
_e = effective_policy(CHAIN, POLICIES)
assert _e["allowedRegions"] == ("eu", "folder:prod")
assert _e["disableSAKeys"] == ("true", "org")
assert set(_e) == {"allowedRegions", "disableSAKeys"}
_e2 = effective_policy(["org"], {"org": {"x": "1"}})
assert _e2 == {"x": ("1", "org")}
_e3 = effective_policy(["org", "p"], {"org": {"x": "1"}, "p": {"x": "2"}})
assert _e3["x"] == ("2", "p")

q.en: What is the fundamental difference between IAM and Organization Policy?

en: Org Policy is the paid tier of IAM
IAM controls what identities can do; Org Policy restricts what resources/configurations may exist, binding even owners
Org Policy only applies to VMs

q.en: The org allows regions us,eu; the prod folder sets eu; the project sets nothing. Effective regions for the project?

en: eu — the nearest ancestor that sets the constraint wins

q.en: Which constraint eliminates the risk of downloadable service account key files?

en: A Google service that sells projects
A Terraform module + CI pipeline that creates projects with baseline IAM, labels, networking, and folder placement via pull request
